title: How a future Bronco hero Jesse Southwell broke Queensland's heart
text:
Just days before beginning her quest to become one of Brisbane's new heroes, Jesse Southwell was Queensland's cruellest heartbreaker.

The New South Wales halfback made the difference in yesterday's State of Origin series opener with a 65th-minute field goal that broke the deadlock en route to an 11-6 victory in Newcastle.

It was the first drop goal the 21-year-old has landed in a game in her entire life and, with Southwell moving from Newcastle to Brisbane this very weekend, a fitting farewell to the only rugby league home she has ever known.

The two plays that flipped the script on Women's State of Origin Game I With Queensland on the verge of another classic ambush, it took two "Origin plays" for New South Wales to turn the tide in the women's series opener in Newcastle.

"I've never kicked a field goal in a real game before. It was (Olivia) Kernick; we had a discussion earlier about setting up for a field goal," Southwell said.

"She told me to go for the first one. I missed it, and the second one, she said, 'Jesse, kick it now!' I was like, 'sweet' and I had lots of time.

"I don't think I've ever kicked one in any game. It was a good time to start.

"But you practice them all the time, so you know what's coming. You can put that kind of pressure on yourself at training; six-all, in a State of Origin game, minute to go, you have to get it to win. You put that in your brain when you kick it at training.

"I move on Sunday. We have some stuff next week for Bronx. I've been flying up and back."

The field goal will be Southwell's last rugby league act in the Hunter until who knows when — Brisbane are not scheduled to play Newcastle away in this year's NRLW regular season.

But despite Southwell leading the Knights to two premierships, capturing Origin glory at home was a longtime coming, given Queensland had won the previous two matches in the Hunter.

"It was really cool, really surreal, to be at home and have a good game with the girls and break that streak. We've (NSW) lost here a few times, so it's good," Southwell said.

Jesse Southwell is the NRLW's future, and the heart of Newcastle's all-conquering present Having led the Newcastle Knights to an NRLW premiership at just 17 years old, Newcastle's Jesse Southwell has the potential to be the NRLW's face for a decade, writes Nick Campton.

"It was still five minutes left, and I knew the game wasn't over yet. Queensland have done it to New South Wales in the men's and the women's time and time again.

"I was just thinking about my next job, and not losing the game from there."

The pressure on Southwell in Brisbane, which is coming off a premiership win of its own, will be immense, but that is nothing new for her.

Since she burst onto the scene in 2022 as a 17-year-old, expectations have always been heightened when it comes to her career.

She is among the first of a generation of players who are pure footballers, and she has it in her to be the best of them.

A game like yesterday's, where chances were hard to come by, but Southwell came up with the key play when it counted most, is as important a part of the journey as the masterclasses.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

It is also proof that for Southwell, be it for New South Wales or Brisbane, and despite her many achievements thus far, the best is yet to come.

"I think we have a lot to improve on. Personally, as the half, there's a lot I can clean up," Southwell said.

"But that's our first game of the year, our first hit out. Everyone wanted a three-game series, and we've got it, so there's a lot of cool improvement to come.

"We knew at half-time that we were actually on top. The scoreboard didn't show that, but we knew we were on top.

"The coaching staff had a lot of faith in us, the group had a lot of faith in each other. We know what we can do; we just had to stay in the grind."

title: Footage of Daniels's NBA fight sparks criticism of Aussie star
text:
Atlanta's Australian star Dyson Daniels has been ejected from Game 6 of the Hawks-Knicks NBA play-off series after sparking a melee.

Mitchell Robinson boxed out Daniels, who responded with a right elbow to the chest of his Knicks opponent.

The incident followed a pair of free throws from Anunoby that gave the Knicks a 50-point lead in the second quarter.

A scuffle ensued, sending an official to the floor as players from both teams got involved.

The Hawks' Onyeka Okongwu and Knicks' Jalen Brunson held Robinson back as the fight inched toward fans sitting courtside.

Nickeil Alexander-Walker and several coaches pulled Daniels away, including Hawks head coach Quin Snyder.

Players from both teams became involved in the altercation. (AP: Brynn Anderson)

Robinson was also ejected from the game.

The Knicks won comfortably 140-89 to claim a 4-2 series victory and eliminate the Hawks from the play-offs.

Daniels did not receive much support on X after footage of the incident was posted.

One X user referred to Daniels as the "Great Barrier Loser", which is a play on his nickname "Great Barrier Thief".

Another user referred to Daniels's behaviour as "pathetic".

The Hawks' 83-36 deficit was the largest at half-time in NBA play-off history.

It was second overall, when including regular-season games, trailing only Dallas' 77-27 lead over the Los Angeles Clippers in 2020. The Mavericks went on to win 124-73.

The Knicks will face the winner of the Boston Celtics and Philadelphia 76ers series in the Eastern Conference semifinals.

ABC/AP

title: Australian screen industry as 'intergenerational' as housing
text:
Preserving Australia's film and TV industry is as important as fixing the housing crisis or saving the environment.

That was one of the key messages from the 2026 Screen Forever conference, which saw key decision-makers gather on the Gold Coast to discuss the future of the industry.

"At a time when social cohesion is under significant pressure, our stories … are even more important than ever, particularly for younger generations," Screen Producers Australia CEO Matthew Deaner said, while opening the conference.

" This is as much about intergenerational equity as housing or the environment. Australian voices and Australian stories [are] worth celebrating, worth protecting, worth preserving and we are its custodians. "

The conference, now in its 40th year, opened on a high with a celebration of the Communications Legislation Amendment Bill, which passed through parliament at the end of 2025.

Industry welcomes content quotas for streamers Arts Minister Tony Burke has proposed a requirement for 10 per cent of streaming services' local expenditure to be spent on Australian film and TV. Here's what the industry thinks.

The new law forces all streamers operating in Australia with over 1 million subscribers — in 2026 this includes AppleTV, Disney+, HBO Max, Netflix, Paramount+, Prime Video and Stan — to commit to reinvesting at least 10 per cent of their total program expenditure for Australia in local productions.

"Now, it's fair to say that local content rules on streaming platforms took far too long and created unhelpful friction within our industry," Deaner said, impressing that while the laws have been passed, it would still be some time before the effect is felt by the industry and audiences.

"But I do think there's an opportunity, and an opportunity to sort of set things forward in a different way within our industry, given the battles before us."

At the box office

Australians cinemas are finally feeling a bounce back after COVID caused the industry to close overnight; the 2025 box office took in almost $1 billion dollars in a near-return to pre-pandemic levels.

It's a trend which is continuing in 2026.

Hoyts CEO Damian Keogh said the Australian box office was already up 14 per cent from this time last year.

"That's an extra 1.5 million people going on cinema visits from January to now," he said.

"The level of optimism in the cinema industry is certainly way higher than I've seen since the COVID days."

Hoyts CEO Damian Keogh (right) and Madman's Paul Wiegard at Screen Forever 40. (Supplied: Screen Producers Australia)

Keogh also said Hoyts had pre-sold more tickets to the recently released The Devil Wears Prada 2 than they did during the Barbie phenomenon in 2023 — further cementing the theory that audiences are no longer going to the movies but going to see a movie.

But while big Hollywood blockbusters and IP-forward content are creating event-watching, it remains a struggle for independent and Australian productions to crack through.

Madman CEO Paul Wiegard said local film made up just 2 per cent of the Australian box office.

"It's a very different landscape to where we were 10 years ago, in terms of just the volume of content. [The amount] of titles rolling through the doors is enormous," Wiegard said.

"Audiences turn out for singular, bold films from directors. We need to do more backing to let the artists do their thing.

"If you looked at the films at the Academy Awards this year, they were bold, singular works across the board. We need artistically led projects in addition to the broad-based family projects."

Flicking channels

While the effects of the streaming quota bill will likely not be seen for a couple of years, the legislation also included a $50 million funding boost for the ABC to put towards drama and children's programs over the next three years.

"The ABC at the moment, in terms of audiences, we're seeing real growth. The best thing for me is that Australian content is what audiences want," ABC's head of Screen Jennifer Collins said.

Collins and Foxtel and Binge director of entertainment Daniel Monaghan emphasised the need to be "noisy" in an increasingly loud landscape.

Jen Collins (middle) with Daniel Monaghan and moderator Virginia Trioli at Screen Forever 40. (Supplied: Screen Producers Australia)

"We don't want noise for noise sakes. It's not about shocking people. It's about telling a story authentically," Monaghan said.

He confirmed Binge was trying to widen its male audience.

"If you are chasing men, for instance, be unapologetic about the stories that you're telling. Don't worry if that's not for someone who's already on the platform," he said,

Collins and Monaghan expressed a desire to see more Australian comedy on their platforms.

At the other end of the streaming spectrum, Haven't You Done Well general manager Georgia Mappin is celebrating the successful launch of streaming service Grouse House.tv in April.

"I have 1,000 subscribers and they're paying for the content that they can get for free. I think that's what's cool about my 1,000 subscribers," Mappin told ABC News.

Built off the work of Australian comedy group Aunty Donna, Haven't You Done Well has branched out into shows and web series from a variety of comedians.

The production house's bypassing of the gatekeepers of traditional screen has allowed them to cultivate a growing, dedicated audience.

Haven't You Done Well Production's Georgia Mappin (left) in conversation with Screen Queensland's Julie Eckersley. (Supplied: Screen Producers Australia)

"Everyone is still talking about how we drive audience over to broadcast and I'm like, no, it's not about driving. It's about accessing audiences where they're consuming and that doesn't work in the traditional model," Mappin said.

With former partnerships with the ABC and Netflix, Mappin says Haven't You Done Well wasn't opposed to collaboration with traditional media but it wouldn't define the operation.

"We make content everywhere [our audience are] consuming the content," Mappin said.

"That's why our production company is platform agnostic, but we're genre specialists."

The future of screen in Australia

When asked what the future of content at the ABC is, managing director Hugh Marks had a firm response: Intellectual property, or IP.

"It's all about intellectual property. It's about us, and the industry, taking the right steps to create the IP that's going to create the value that's going to be able to be reinvested into our market," Marks said.

Marks also touched on his frustration at the speed at which content is produced in the industry at the moment.

ABC managing director Hugh Marks spoke on a Screen Forever 40 panel about the future of the Australian industry. (Supplied: Screen Producers Australia)

"It can be three, four, five years between what's a great idea to realisation of that onto Australian screen," he said.

"We need to bring these ideas, the ones that we really love, through faster."

Sitting on the same panel, Screen Australia's Director of First Nations Strategy and filmmaker Rachel Perkins started her peek into the future by looking at the progress which has been made.

First Nations film Wolfram writes hope into heavy history Convinced to return to the story of Sweet Country, Kaytetye filmmaker Warwick Thornton fell in love with the possibilities.

"Twenty years ago, I look at where the Indigenous film industry was at and it was really out of nowhere," Perkins said.

"Now, we've got Goolagong doing fabulously on the ABC. We've got Wolfram releasing this week. We have Kangaroo, which is co-produced by an Indigenous production company.

"All across the industry, we're really seeing that we have cemented our place in the industry as core to Australian identity and storytelling, which is really to be, you know, something to be really proud of."

For Perkins, the future looks like sustaining this growth and solidifying the industry with support for emerging creators.

"We have to look at the success of the past, what we've changed through being organised, working collectively, looking at government policy, pulling those levers to really chart the future," she said.

"That's a different proposal from what you're talking about in terms of IP, but similar in terms of ensuring that Indigenous content creators, like all Australian content creators, have sustainable careers and that those markets are available to us and that they're guaranteed."

Rachel Perkins with Big and Little Films co-founder Tony Ayres. (Supplied: Screen Producers Australia)

One of the First Nations talents looking to the future is Caden Pearson, a filmmaker and director of Resonant Films, who was crowned one of Screen Producers Australia's One To Watch 2025.

In a reflection of the time it can take to build a profile; Pearson has been in the industry for 12 years but landing on One To Watch was like a "spotlight".

"It's an ultra-marathon in that, unless you're really, really lucky, you don't make it overnight," Pearson told ABC News.

"I have noticed that people that have been achieving things and been in it for a long time, a common theme that I've noticed is that we all have a weird, strong self-belief.

"If you're going to get to where you want to be you have to believe. I know I will get there if I just keep going.

"This must be something, otherwise we would all give up."

title: Coaches and fans grumble as technology scrutiny rises
text:
In line with the increased professionalism in sport is a drive to be correct as much as possible.

For many, close enough isn't good enough. Seeing your team "robbed" is one of the most heartbreaking experiences a fan can live through.

Last weekend, the concept of correct was tested to the extremes on two prominent occasions. On Sunday afternoon, 60 seconds of football was forgotten to the annals of time at Docklands.

That was the time taken between Rowan Marshall's mark on the line and the umpire blowing the whistle in the middle of the ground signalling a score review had been undertaken.

A few hours later, at Canberra's Manuka Oval, another score review stopped the game in its tracks.

GWS' Xavier O'Halloran booted what in the moment seemed like the match-sealing goal before doubt clouded the ground.

Match-sealer? Let us check that. (Getty Images: AFL Photos/Mark Metcalfe)

Eighty-five seconds passed between the ball sailing through the posts and the ball being bounced, with players shuffling up and down the ground preparing for a variety of options in the interim.

Unlike the earlier game, O'Halloran's goal was upheld.

The reaction to these incidents was swift from AFL HQ.

"They got that decision correct [Marshall's mark], but it obviously took too long," AFL executive general manager of football operations Greg Swann said on Monday.

AFL boss says umpiring 'as good as it's been' AFL football boss Greg Swann says most of the feedback he receives from the clubs surrounding the officiating in the league is positive despite a pair of controversial review calls in Round 7.

"Going forward now the ARC won't do that. The ARC will still do goal reviews, but they won't intervene in a score like they did on the weekend unless the goal umpire asks for a score review."

The change is the latest in the long line of adjustments the league has made to its score review system since its introduction 15 years ago.

When introduced at the time, then-AFL general manager of football operations Adrian Anderson was clear about the operation of the video assistance.

"Video technology will not remove all errors from the game, as some replays cannot assist, but the aim is to improve decision-making where possible," Anderson said.

At the start, Anderson said that there would be only one to two reviews per game, each causing delays of no more than a minute.

Since then, the system has evolved dramatically.

Instead of obvious errors being the focus, marginal decisions both at and away from the goal line were increasingly being considered.

On at least five separate occasions, changes to the scope or process of score reviews were made during a season, often in the shadow of finals.

These changes often have addressed recent events, such as Adelaide's finals miss in 2023, but not what drives the use of the system and its overall merits.

Criticism of the use of video review in sport, not just the AFL, usually focuses on two elements: the suitability of the technology or the overall need for the assistance and scope for its use.

North Melbourne coach Alastair Clarkson and Giants counterpart Adam Kingsley addressed these issues after GWS' narrow seven-point win.

"I'm not sure it's up to speed yet," Kingsley said.

"It doesn't feel like we're ahead of other sports."

Clarkson said: "I'd prefer there was none [technology] and we just leave it up to the umpires to adjudicate."

Skepticism is growing in sport around video review, and the question of its proper role and scope remains unresolved.

To err is human?

Umpires and players are always going to disagree over calls on-field. (Getty Images: AFL Photos/Michael Willson)

Sport can be complex, with lengthy and detailed rule books. For the most part, things are kept moving by expert adjudicators such as referees or umpires, making decisions on the fly.

But a small subset of decisions are difficult, impactful, controversial and even incorrect.

It's these judgements that are often the trigger for sporting bodies to deploy technology to assist those making rulings on the field. The so-called "howlers" should obviously be corrected, right?

This is an often-repeated justification for the introduction of technological aids such as the AFL's score review system and cricket's Decision Review System (DRS).

In 2019, then-AFL football operations boss Stephen Hocking told SEN: "The score review system was introduced to correct obvious scoring errors."

But how many errors do umpires really make?

Research undertaken by Deakin University showed AFL umpires got about 78 per cent of calls right based on data from the 2016 season.

That may seem high to many fans, but most decisions on the field are not controversial or noteworthy. When umpires got it wrong, they were about twice as likely to miss a call as to pay a free kick erroneously.

This is a stable long-term pattern with earlier data from the AFL in 2000 showing effectively the same accuracy rate.

This 78 per cent accuracy rate for paying free kicks awarded also stacks up reasonably well in a global context, given that research also tends to show physical distance and physical fatigue impact decision-making, factors particularly relevant to Australian football.

The use of technology in sport is designed to reduce this rate where possible. Often the identification of howlers is devolved to players or coaches. Their success rate in doing so is less than ideal.

When given the chance to directly challenge decisions, players and coaches are not significantly better at judging calls.

Such review systems are often about playing the percentages rather than rectifying clear errors. Increasingly, video review is used to not just review the obvious howlers but also tight decisions made at the margins.

West Coast coach Andrew McQualter had an immediate view after his own experiences on Sunday.

"I just think that if they're going to do it, it's got to be clear-cut," he said.

"They've got to get it right. If it's touch and go I would think you're not changing the flow of a game to make a decision like that."

There is also data suggesting that sometimes when a video official intervenes, they make the wrong decision or should not have intervened at all.

According to reporting by the BBC, there were 13 errors made by the video assistant referee (VAR) in the first half of this English Premier League season.

Referees in the Premier League are being put under even more pressure by poor VAR decisions. (Getty Images: Alex Livesey)

These errors, and the delays taken for reviews, have had an impact on fan confidence in the system, with a March survey of 202 having shown that 81 per cent of EPL fans prefer to watch games without VAR.

Academic research also shows that sports officials are quite skilled at getting the easy, more objective calls correct. Unfortunately, it is far more likely that close calls are reviewed than the obvious decisions.

This level of accuracy in both original decision-making and the success of reviews has led to some people questioning the value of these systems.

Clarkson's comments after Sunday's match led to this type of logic.

"There's mistakes by players, there's mistakes by umpires," he said.

"The ball goes through whether it's touched by a fingernail or not, who cares really? Whether it snicks the post or not, who cares really? Just make the call.

"[It's] swings and roundabouts. You are going to win some and you're going to lose some. There seems to be just as much conjecture now as what there always was."

Coaches are starting to wish umpires would stick with their original calls. (Getty Images: AFL Photos/Daniel Carson)

Other coaches, such as Luke Beveridge and Damien Hardwick, have echoed this sentiment.

Some fans, players and coaches wish for no use of additional technology, while others advocate for a reduced scope of adoption.

In 2018, then-AFL chief executive Gillon McLachlan was somewhat sympathetic to those feelings in the wake of yet another score review controversy.

"It's not, 'Scrap the whole thing'," McLachlan said at the time.

"It's do we actually finesse it a bit or tone it down and stop trying to solve stuff, which is too hard to do, like a touch from 50 metres, so we'll limit its purview?

"If you did that, then that's a fair conversation. Getting rid of it is not."

There is also the question of whether the technology involved in assisting the umpires is right for the job right now.

Sport, lies and videotape

Fans are increasingly frustrated by the use of technology. (Getty Images: Carl Recine)

Watching the footy, from either the stands or the couch, is a pleasure most of us undertake on a weekly basis.

But sometimes we do not see the whole picture.

Many ordinary sport viewers perhaps do not quite understand the extent to which seemingly infallible video footage can create optical illusions. At a fundamental level, video is a two-dimensional rendering of three-dimensional reality, and certainty is elusive.

Lens zoom and camera angles are big culprits. Cricket viewers are familiar with the dilemma of judging low catches from broadcast footage due to the "foreshortening" effect of a strong zoom making grass, hand and ball nearly impossible to place in three-dimensional space.

Australian football has its own version of this with contentious out-of-bounds judgements around the curved pockets.

Viewers may try forlornly to judge if a ball, while in the air, crossed an imaginary plane over a curved boundary line a few centimetres wide. Often the answer here is simply impossible to discern. As a result, highlights such as Barry Breen's famous point in the 1966 grand final may have been nullified with incorrect use of technology.

Video frame rates and shutter speed also present significant issues for the task of conveying an approximation of reality to an audience.

Consider a football flying quickly towards goal being adjudicated on whether it crossed the back of the goal line before being touched by a defender.

Standard frame rates and shutter speeds can often render balls, arms and digits into a blur, missing the critical moment completely.

Even the high-speed cameras used by the ARC in limited situations last year can struggle to capture the precise moment of a ball crossing the 5-centimetre goal line. Fans and officials often look for signals such as fingers bending back to signal the ball being touched, often without definitive clarity.

Australian football is unique in the way it is played on a large field, with a fast-moving ball and no offside rule or line of scrimmage. These elements make it harder to track the ball than in most sports.

Kingsley touched on issues surrounding technology when speaking after Sunday's match.

Although supportive of the use of technology, Kingsley questioned the current implementation.

"Whether it's the goal line cameras, [or] whether the ball isn't fully across the line … I don't know," Kingsley said.

"[We need to] keep trying to develop as best as you can. You're not going to start with the finished product, so just progressively try and get better and better."

It took a while for Rowan Marshall's mark against the Eagles to be cleared. (Getty Images: AFL Photos/Michael Willson)

Swann indicated in the preseason the league was looking at introducing even more high-frame rate cameras in the coming years.

"We're looking now to upgrade the cameras," Swann told SEN in February.

"There are new cameras out there that we are looking to either purchase or lease that [are] six times better."

There is also more advanced tracking used in some sports, most famously the Hawk-Eye system used in sports such as tennis and cricket.

The hawkeye system, used in cricket and tennis, measures to the millimetre. (Getty Images: Alex Livesey)

These systems use synchronised, fixed cameras to minimise video shortcomings and statistically estimate a trajectory.

Their mere millimetres of error are often cited in their favour, but those error margins mean these systems are responsible for creating an entirely false sense of accuracy and precision.

The Hawk-Eye system works by statistically estimating a ball's position or trajectory based on multiple cameras, but the standard visualisation shows a single location.

A tennis ball might be shown two millimetres out and viewers have no hint that this represents a statistical tie, a null result on a significance test.

The system genuinely cannot say if this ball was out or not.

And that is under ideal conditions. Both cricket and tennis have seen documented situations where larger errors occur due to weather conditions like heat, wind and unusual ball spin.

Cricket's DRS system accounts for this to some degree in its interpretation of LBW decisions, creating an "umpire's call" zone where decisions cannot be confidently predicted.

But few other sports have followed this lead, either in the deployment of Hawk-Eye or other video review systems.

Soccer's notorious VAR renders margins of error into visual certainty even more blatantly. Current applications of off-side technology (SOAT) may have error margins of several centimetres, depending on player speed and the original starting frame for computation.

The EPL has even introduced a five-centimetre buffer to account for these potential errors. It is an error range FIFA has not yet adopted.

Other technologies have also been tested for broader use. The AFL has trialled the use of microchipped balls, while other sports have implemented trials using local and global positioning systems.

Many of these efforts have shown limited success to date. But the use of embedded microphones, such as AFL's Edge and cricket's Snicko, have been far more accepted and successful.

This technological advancement, however, may not be enough to silence all of the doubters.

Moving forward

It is rare for technological advancements to be completely removed once introduced, but it is not uncommon for their use to be constrained or expanded further.

Nevertheless, there is a sizeable cohort of fans and officials calling for further review of video review.

Is all the extra noise around umpire reviews worth it? (Getty Images: AFL Photos/Russell Freeman)

"The game is chaotic, it's not black and white," Beveridge told reporters this week.

"Ultimately, if there's confusion as to who it went off, what did we used to do? We used to ball it in.

"How about we just ball it in again? People are going to make mistakes. And it might have come off someone's boot.

"But no, we have to bring another layer in, and now we have to reverse it."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Simplification and a tolerance of uncertainty may be hard to enact, but many fans, players and officials have a desire to return to the game from their youth, which did not have score review.

There is a serious argument for the game to be decided on the field by those there at the time and let uncertainty be part of the experience.

Doubting the judgement of on-field umpires also has flow-on effects to the trust in their non-reviewable decisions.

Some studies involving elite football players have even indicated players feel a sense of frustration and anxiety relating to the constant monitoring by VAR.

Some players mentioned they felt referees had been disempowered by the use of technology.

Beyond this nostalgia-driven desire, there are real questions surrounding the scope of video review and the problems — howlers or general calls — that the system is trying to resolve.

The AFL will have to work hard to find a middle ground to placate all of its stakeholders in the years to come.

title: Stuart tells Bellamy to put himself first after shock neurological diagnosis
text:
Canberra coach Ricky Stuart has thrown his support behind his good friend Craig Bellamy after the Melbourne mentor was diagnosed with an unspecified neurological degenerative condition.

Bellamy will remain as the Storm coach for the immediate future after the club revealed last night that the 66-year-old underwent tests and was diagnosed with the disorder.

Melbourne did not provide any further details of the diagnosis.

Stuart and Bellamy are two of the NRL's closest coaches, having played for the Raiders in the late 80s and early 90s, including the 1990 grand final win over Penrith.

Ricky Stuart and Craig Bellamy talk during an Australian Kangaroos training session at Olympic Park in 2006. (Getty Images: Aaron Francis)

Their friendship has continued to today, with the pair linking up most recently when the Raiders beat the Storm 26-22 on April 17.

Before their round seven clash, Stuart declared that Bellamy is the best coach in the modern era and that he holds him in high regard.

When asked whether he was aware of his friend's situation, Stuart said he knew about Bellamy's condition about three or four weeks ago.

"I know he's getting very good advice (and) got good people around him … in Melbourne," Stuart said.

"He's got a beautiful family, so it's now time for Craig to really care about Craig and put himself first."

In February, Bellamy signed a contract with the Storm until the end of the 2028 season.

The 66-year-old has coached 614 NRL games and boasts a win rate of almost 70 per cent, while he also played 150 matches for Canberra.

Melbourne is enduring a horror season, dropping six successive matches for the first time since Bellamy took over as coach in 2003.

A seventh-straight loss in Brisbane tonight to the Dolphins would equal the all-time club record set in 2002.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AAP

title: Tense moment at FIFA Congress as delegate refuses 'absurd' handshake request
text:
Palestine Football Association president Jibril Rajoub has refused to shake hands with Basim Sheikh Suliman, the vice-president of Israel's soccer governing body, during a tense moment at the FIFA Congress.

After the two men addressed the congress, they were called on stage by FIFA president Gianni Infantino. They stood far apart from each other, and Rajoub protested loudly away from the microphones before leaving the stage, as Infantino tried to bring the duo together.

Asked what Rajoub said when he refused, Palestinian FA vice-president Susan Shalabi, who was in the room, told Reuters: "I cannot shake the hand of someone the Israelis have brought to whitewash their fascism and genocide! We are suffering."

Speaking before the congress, Rajoub called on FIFA to address the Palestine Football Association's (PFA) allegations that Israel has breached anti-discrimination regulations by allowing clubs to be based in the West Bank settlements.

He confirmed that the PFA is taking the matter to the Court of Arbitration for Sport after FIFA ruled in March not to suspend Israel over its West Bank clubs. FIFA cited the unresolved and complex legal status of the West Bank.

Speaking to Reuters after the congress ended, Shalabi said Infantino's attempt to have Suliman and Rajoub shake hands showed little consideration for the Palestinian FA chief's speech, in which he made yet another plea for Israeli clubs not to base teams in ⁠the West Bank settlements.

"To be put in a position ⁠where to have a handshake after everything that was said, this negates the whole purpose of the speech that the general [Rajoub] was giving," she said.

Gianni Infantino attempts to calm Jibril Rajoub after the Palestinian refused the handshake request. (Getty Images: Rich Lam)

"He spent like 15 minutes trying to explain to everyone how ⁠the rules matter, how this could easily become a precedent where the rights of member associations are violated with impudence, ⁠and then we'll just wrap this under the carpet. ⁠It was absurd."

In a separate matter involving an Israeli club, FIFA fined the Israel Football Association (IFA) $US190,000 ($264,000) on disciplinary charges relating to "discrimination and racist abuse," plus "offensive behaviour and violations of the principles of fair play".

After the two men left the stage at the Vancouver Convention Centre, Infantino thanked them for addressing the delegates and made an appeal.

"President Rajoub, vice-president Suliman, let's work together. Let's work together to give hope to the children. Let's work together for that," Infantino said.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Following the congress, Rajoub gave an impassioned plea, asking whether Israel has "the right to even be part of FIFA".

"From my side, I still respect and follow all the legal procedures through FIFA institutions, but I think it's time to understand that Israel should be sanctioned because of the violations of the statutes of FIFA, the human rights," he said.

Yariv Teper, acting general secretary of the IFA, would not comment on the specifics of Rajoub's comments but said the IFA would be willing to work with the Palestinian counterparts.

"We are in the FIFA Congress," Teper said. "Our mission is to promote football and a better future for all regions, and this is our mission."

Palestinian soccer officials have long argued, including at FIFA annual congresses across the past 15 years, before Infantino was president, that Israel violates statutes by letting teams from settlements in the West Bank play in Israel's national league.

The disciplinary investigation of Israeli soccer was also opened 18 months ago in response to a second objection by the Palestinian federation.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AP/Reuters

title: SA government needs certainty on LIV Golf by October, premier says
text:
South Australia's premier says the government is not yet considering putting a stop to its North Adelaide Golf Course upgrade, amid uncertainty over the future of the LIV Golf tournament.

However, he said the government was "monitoring" the situation closely and would need a guarantee of its future in Adelaide by October this year.

The Saudi Arabia government confirmed overnight that it will withdraw its multi-billion dollar backing of LIV Golf at the end of the 2026 season.

The Public Investment Fund (PIF) released a statement stating that the "substantial investment" required by the event over a longer term is "no longer consistent with the current phase of its investment strategy".

It's left the breakaway tour searching for the funds to keep going.

Australia's Cameron Smith is one of several LIV Golf players who refused a PGA Tour offer to return to the circuit. (Getty Images: Jan Kruger)

The state government had just begun a controversial $45 million redevelopment of the North Adelaide Golf Course to eventually host the popular tournament.

The uncertainty has left the premier needing to make decisions, including on the future of more than 500 trees that were going to be removed at the golf course.

"Well look, LIV hasn't been cancelled so I think … we'd be putting the cart before the horse if we just, sort of, decided to cancel the golf course when LIV very much intend to be here into the future," he told 891 ABC Radio Adelaide.

"Now, let's wait and see how that plays out."

Trees are expected to be cut down as part of the North Adelaide golf course redevelopment. (ABC News: Brant Cumming)

Mr Malinauskas said works had already begun but they had some time to monitor the tournament's future.

"The trees aren't being chopped down for a while yet so that does buy time, yes," he said.

"But there are works that are already happening and that will continue in situ.

"As it stands right now, what do we know? We know that LIV Golf intend to be here next year."

Despite the concerns, Mr Malinauskas said the planned upgrades stacked up even without the breakaway tournament.

"Since the late 90s, the Adelaide City Council have wanted to do this to the golf course in the city," he said.

A protester against the North Adelaide golf course redevelopment. (ABC News: Angelique Donnellan)

"No one's ever had the capacity to actually bring it to fruition. We're bringing it to fruition ultimately for the benefit of community use.

"Now, of course it is better if we have a major event in the heart of the city … but ultimately this is an investment for people and there is also an economic underpinning with golf tourism on the rise as well."

But, there's also been a lot of opposition to the works going ahead from his political opponents.

"Without having an event like LIV Golf, it's hard to see how $45 million is justifiable," Liberal leader Ashton Hurn said yesterday.

"This is taxpayer funds at a time where we've got record debt in South Australia, we've got emergency departments right across regional SA that are closing, and people are crying out for cost-of-living relief."

Greens MLC Robert Simms said it was not too late for the government to change course and "stop the chop".

"The business case for this redevelopment has totally evaporated," Mr Simms said yesterday.

'We want it to continue'

Mr Malinauskas admitted he was concerned with the unknown surrounding the tournament's future.

The SA Premier says LIV Golf CEO Scott O'Neil is planning for the tournament to continue. (Getty Images: Hector Vivas)

However, he'd been assured by the LIV Golf chief executive Scott O'Neil that they planned to go ahead.

"They certainly are working around the clock to make sure they continue on into the future," he said.

"As far as South Australia's concerned, particularly given the unqualified success this event has been economically for our state, that's a good thing.

"Having said that, I'm not naive to the fact that these reports have been going on for a good, you know, almost a fortnight and it's something we continue to monitor very closely."

He said the SA government had a timeline for when it will need to know future events are guaranteed.

"First thing is, the way the South Australian government contract with LIV Golf operates is we pay a fee for them to host an event," he said.

"Now if they don't host an event we don't pay the fee. It's pretty simple.

"And of course the fee that we pay LIV Golf to put on that event is a very small portion of the overall cost of hosting the event.

"All in all, I think, everyone acknowledges it's been a success, we want it to continue into the future.

"But we won't be paying them to host an event they that don't put on.

"We'll have to have a clear indication on this probably no later than, sort of, October."

title: Poliovirus detected in Perth wastewater
text:
Poliovirus has been detected in a sample of Perth's wastewater, health officials have revealed.

Authorities said the detection in mid-April was evidence of a vaccine-derived poliovirus Type 2 strain, similar to what has been detected in countries such as Africa, Europe and Papua New Guinea in recent years.

Western Australia's chief health officer, Dr Clare Huppatz, said while it was extremely unusual, it presented a very low risk to the population.

"While this is a significant finding, this detection does not provide evidence of spread of the disease in WA," she said.

"The potential for this strain to circulate in a highly vaccinated population is very low, and the poliovirus vaccination coverage in WA children is 92 per cent."

Dr Huppatz noted the presence of the virus in wastewater in Europe during 2024 and 2025 did not lead to anyone developing the disease.

What is vaccine-derived poliovirus? The vaccine for poliovirus effectively interrupts person-to-person spread. But on rare occasions in under-immunised populations, the live weakened virus contained in the vaccine can mutate and circulate, according to the Global Polio Eradication Initiative. That results in vaccine-derived poliovirus, as suspected in this case. Australia uses inactivated polio vaccines which do not contain live virus and are given by injection.

“This finding is most likely from someone who has travelled overseas and is shedding this virus strain," she said.

She said the case served as a reminder for people to be fully vaccinated against the disease while it still circulated overseas.

"The best thing they can do is check that they're vaccinated … I wouldn't expect to see this be a risk to the community, it's just something that we really do need to monitor," Dr Huppatz said.

University of New South Wales medical epidemiologist Dr Abrar Chughtai said there had been similar detections in Australia recently.

“Australia previously detected vaccine-derived poliovirus in Melbourne wastewater in 2024, linked to an unvaccinated traveller," he said.

"Similar detections have occurred globally in many countries ... mainly in under-immunised populations, with no subsequent outbreaks."

Testing increased​

The WA Department of Health said it was now increasing the frequency of wastewater poliovirus testing in the Perth metropolitan area.

Dr Huppatz said the virus was detected in the Subiaco wastewater treatment plant which covers Subiaco and the Perth CBD, an area of about 250,000 people.

Dr Clare Huppatz says wastewater testing for poliovirus will be done weekly in the near future. (ABC News: Hugh Sando)

She said people "can actually excrete the virus for quite some time".

"We will continue to test the wastewater in the Subiaco treatment plant but also in the adjacent ones, which cover a lot of the population of Perth," she said.

"We'll test weekly for the next few months to test what the signal shows.

"We will also alert any clinicians to look for any clinical cases, as part of our surveillance."

History of polio in Australia

According to the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW), Australia was declared polio-free in 2000.

A chart has shown a sharp decline in polio cases since 1956, when the vaccine was introduced in Australia. (Supplied: Australia Institute of Health and Welfare)

"Although there has been no known local transmission of the poliovirus in Australia since 1972, there remains a risk of the importation of polio from overseas," its website reads.

Since 1987, the only case of polio detected in Australia was in 2007 when an overseas-born student acquired the disease during a visit to a country with known ongoing polio transmission, according to the AIHW.

The previous two epidemics of polio in Australia were in 1956, and from 1960 to 1962.

Cases of polio sharply declined after 1956, when the vaccine was introduced.

Polio, also known as poliomyelitis, is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the poliovirus.

Polio spreads through contact with infected faeces, leading to gastrointestinal infection.

According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), polio can spread through contaminated water or food, and largely affects children under 5 years of age — although anyone of any age who is unvaccinated can contract the disease.

'Wild' poliovirus cases have decreased by over 99 per cent since 1988, the WHO said, but the virus is still endemic in two countries — Pakistan and Afghanistan.

title: Smelter jobs at stake as government assistance runs dry
text:
A $135 million dollar assistance package for Nyrstar smelters in Port Pirie and Hobart runs out today, leading to concerns about the future of more than 1,000 workers.

Last August, Nyrstar received a rescue package from the federal, South Australian and Tasmanian governments to allow the struggling smelter to continue its operations, but it runs out today.

The South Australian government had contributed $55 million, Tasmania contributed $22.5 million and the federal government provided $57.5 million.

South Australia's Premier Peter Malinauskas said he was "concerned" about the future of the Nyrstar metal smelter in Port Pirie.

Tim Ayres (left) and Peter Malinauskas visited the Port Pirie smelter in February 2026. (ABC News: Jenae Madden)

"We have not yet been able to conclude negotiations for the future of that support package into the future," he told 891 ABC Adelaide.

Mr Malinauskas said that all governments "remain at the negotiating table" to secure the future of operations, but he could not provide any guarantees for workers yet.

"It is not true to say that negotiations have broken down, but it is true to say the negotiations remain ongoing and that was true as recently as yesterday," he said.

Speaking at a press conference on Friday afternoon, Mr Malinauskas said he hoped a "renewed joint government position" would be put to the operators of Nyrstar Port Pirie and Hobart by the end of the day.

"Although it is true I am concerned about Nyrstar's position, it is also true that I am confident that we are moving in the right direction to see a resolution of this matter so as to obtain ongoing operations at the Port Pirie smelter and the sustained effort on that feasibility study work," he said.

He said there were "900 jobs at stake in Port Pirie, 500 in Hobart".

"And that is a major concern. But it doesn't speak to the national imperative here," he said.

Mr Malinauskas said Port Pirie "has to remain an ongoing operation".

"If we lose our smelting operations, we lose our ability to produce critical minerals and metals, which we know the rest of the world needs, particularly like-minded nations," he said.

Tasmanian Premier Jeremy Rockliff and Industry and Innovation Minister Tim Ayers. (ABC News: Kelsey Reid)

Tasmanian Premier Jeremy Rockliff said he met with Mr Malinauskas and the federal minister last night to try and work through a new support package to ensure "the long-term viability" of Nyrstar.

He said the Tasmanian government had already provided $22.5 million in support, with a further $7.5 million on offer from the state.

He said the state and federal governments had worked well together, providing proportional funding, but it was "vital that the federal government do take a leadership role here".

He said many workers were likely feeling anxious but said "we are in your corner".

A spokesperson for Minister for Industry and Innovation Tim Ayres said he was "committed to maintaining" Australia's smelting industry.

Nyrstar Port Pirie general manager Darrin Cooper said "all options" for the future of the company were being considered.

"Over the past nine months we have delivered important upgrades and commenced production of the nation's first antimony metal in partnership with government," he said in a statement.

"Disappointingly, despite this progress, as of today we have not been able to reach agreement on the next phase and now have to consider all options for the business."

Federal Trade Minister Don Farrell says he hopes for the continued operation of both smelters. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

Federal Trade Minister Don Farrell said local metal smelting capabilities were "absolutely vital" to the national interest and negotiations were ongoing at federal and state levels.

"I'm hopeful that those discussions will result in the continued operation of both of those plants," he said.

"In this modern world, you need to ensure that you have the ability to smelt products."

The Australian Workers' Union SA branch secretary, Gary Henderson, said it was not the first time workers had "been through this situation".

He said the announcement has caused undue stress for the more than 800 smelter employees working at Port Pirie.

"You don't need this type of noise going around," he said.

He said another round of funding was needed to "get the smelter up and running" and all four parties need to "sit down and nut out the deal".

"Workers need a deal to be struck so they can put it to bed and move on," he said.

A stack of zinc ingots at Nyrstar in Hobart, pictured in 2021. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Second phase of support package 'always expected'

In a statement, Tasmanian independent MP Andrew Wilkie said he was "deeply concerned" about the future of the 500 workers employed at Nyrstar's Hobart zinc smelter.

Nyrstar in Hobart operates as a large zinc smelter in the state's south. (ABC News: Maren Preuss)

Mr Wilkie said a "second phase [of the assistance package] was always expected," but a "disagreement over the continuing implementation is putting the package at risk".

He said he has been in touch with Nystar today.

"The survival of the Hobart zinc works is not only important for the hundreds of people it employs and their families, it is also critical for Australia's broader economic resilience and national security," the statement said.

"All four parties need to sort out any disagreements urgently."

Mr Wilkie told 936 ABC Hobart that the $135 million in assistance package agreed last year was always intended to be part of a multi-phase funding bailout, and was always due to be spent by "about now".

"It is a mystery why the negotiations over what I'll call phase two funding has broken down or at least it's going so slowly that there's no guarantee of funding come today, the first of May," he said.

title: Remembering SA's pain and battles behind the Murray-Darling Basin Plan
text:
The dried lake bed was pale, soft underfoot, and apparently deadly with acid sulphate soils that would spread and poison everything the moment the rains returned.

It's 2009 at the height of the Millennium Drought, I'm a journalist in my first post for The Murray Valley Standard, and poor old Lake Albert is a hot bed for politicians visiting from Canberra.

Malcolm Turnbull, Bob Katter, Peter Garrett, Kevin Rudd, Penny Wong — they've all visited at varying times to see what the fuss is about as they argue over how much water should be allocated for environmental flows under the incoming Murray-Darling Basin Plan.

The Murray-Darling Basin Plan explained Australia's most ambitious environmental reform is about to get a shake-up.

Local farmers and politicians have been showing these people in suits how bad it is, trying to explain that SA is not a bunch of whingers at the bottom of the river system, that they are fruit growers, dairy farmers, fishers, that they talk the same language as those upstream, and their message is, "Fix this, or we're screwed".

And then there's the rest of South Australia, a state almost entirely reliant on the Murray River. It tops up our reservoirs, waters our city, regional centres and towns, supports rainfall catchments that are simply not reliable or big enough to sustain a growing society alone.

The river bed at Goolwa near the Murray Mouth was completely exposed in 2009. (Supplied: Lindy Downing)

But people can have short memories.

Sixteen years have passed since the worst drought in Australia's history crippled a large swathe of the country and sent many people to ruin.

The Murray-Darling Basin Plan (MDBP) was put in place to prevent that happening again, but it is up for review and, with public submissions to a discussion paper closing at 5pm today, it's worth taking a little step back in time to remember how dire things became for SA.

A collapsing system

SA's reliance on the Murray hit critical mass as a drought that began in the eastern states in 2001 (or the late 1990s in some areas) spread here with unseasonably sunny winters and an insidious shortage of rain.

As the decade wore on farmers were in dire straits, animals were being culled, forests were dying, there was talk of carting water into SA — water bottles for a metropolitan population that was on level three water restrictions.

Your toilet flush tells a curious story about Australia's worst drought Some kids had never seen a puddle until the Millennium Drought broke. The decade-long dry spell sweeping Australia delivered more than cracked earth, it changed our relationship with water.

River flows across the border had fallen to just 960 gigalitres per year.

Low water levels below Lock One at Blanchetown had caused riverbanks to collapse, swallowing entire trees and parked cars, and irrigators had started seasons in 2008 and 2009 with the lowest allocations on record — just 2 per cent of their entitlements.

The river ends its 2,530-kilometre journey at the Lower Lakes — lakes Alexandrina and Albert, the Murray Mouth and Coorong lagoons. The Murray Mouth closed over in 2002 and had been dredged for eight years to try and keep salinity flushing out the system.

Lake Alexandrina had fallen to 1.1 metres below sea level and Lake Albert to 1m — both nearly dried up completely.

A collapsed river bank at Murray Bridge in South Australia in February 2009. (ABC News)

About 20,000 hectares of soils across the Lower Lakes were exposed and iron sulfides in the soil oxidised in the presence of oxygen to created mud cakes poisoned with sulphuric acid.

The Murray-Darling Basin Authority (MDBA) said soil and water around the edges of some of these lakes became acidic enough to damage steel.

A dairy region that had provided Adelaide with milk all but vanished as farmers who'd been under siege from rising Lake Albert salinity finally had to sell up when there was no more water to irrigate.

But amid all this, there was hope. The federal Water Act 2007 had charged the Commonwealth and basin states — South Australia, Victoria, Queensland, New South Wales and Canberra territory — with bringing about the most complicated and compromising water management reform the world had ever seen.

This map shows the boundary of the Murray Darling Basin, including the boundaries of the northern and southern basins. (Supplied: Murray-Darling Basin Authority)

It would lead to the MDBP, which would reduce sustainable diversions limits (or the water extracted for human use) via infrastructure upgrades, efficiency projects and strategic, and voluntary water licence buybacks

But there were varying opinions and hostile arguments about how much water needed to be reclaimed for the environment, how that water would be reclaimed, where it came from, and the impacts it would have on irrigation communities.

The MDBA was legally required to balance the river's environmental needs with social and economic factors, ie, the protection of communities that had built a life relying on the river.

The SA water security minister of the era Karlene Maywald and said the plan was one of the country's "most important national reforms because it forced governments to confront an uncomfortable truth: We were taking more water than the system could sustain".

A bitter battle

There is no word to describe just how hard SA fought to keep its own lifeline flowing. Dairy farmers, conservationists, irrigators, politicians from all sides (some louder than others) seemed to be in a losing battle against a larger population base in the eastern states that simply could not understand the need to allow precious water to flow into the Lower Lakes.

The River Murray is part of the Murray-Darling Basin, the interconnected system of rivers and lakes that stretches across eastern and southern Australia. (ABC News: Will Hunter)

The drought broke in 2010 as the arguments were still raging. Desalination plants were being built in SA and interstate in response to the drought.

Now it was about the future — building a resilient system for the next drought and the drought after that.

I was writing for The Stock Journal on the night former federal water minister Tony Burke in 2012 came to a Riverland community consultation session and heard an impassioned bunch of very logical farmers and horticulturists argue their case.

I could almost see the exact moment it finally clicked for him — we weren't a bunch of greenies; we just wanted the system to stay alive and usable for all — and when it came to water efficiency, no-one did it better than SA because, well, it's bloody dry and we've never had a choice.

A breakdown of the MDB Plan's water recovery targets. (ABC: Sharon Gordon)

The states would return 2,750 gigalitres of water used by humans back to the system — much less than modelling that had consistently called for 4,000 gigalitres — but an additional 450 gigalitres would be recovered for enhanced environmental outcomes by the end of 2027.

This, the MDBA said, would answer scientists' calls to reduce salinity in the Lower Lakes, keep the river connected with wetlands and flood plains, restore the health of internationally significant Ramsar wetlands, flush algae and salinity from the river, support native fish and birdlife, and ultimately improve overall water quality throughout the entire system.

Review underway

There have been plenty of droughts pocketed throughout the system since the MDBP was established in 2012, but nothing on the level Australians experienced between 2001 and 2010.

The plan has not yet been tested under a comparable drought but it will be, possibly sooner rather than later if climate change modelling is accurate.

Bunds like this at Clayton were constructed during the drought to prevent the widespread acidification of drying river beds. (Supplied: Beth Nixon)

Right now the plan is under review and discussion paper submissions will end on Friday at 5pm. There are improvements that need to be made, tweaking is required, and with just 221of that 450 gigalitres having been recovered by December 2025, we are still a long way off securing the extra water that scientists believe we need to keep the river environment healthy.

Amid this, there are ongoing criticisms about the complexity of the plan, how decisions are getting made, whether governments are delivering their promises on improving water efficiency, and whether the plan's impact on regional communities has been disproportionate in some areas.

As well as addressing these issues, Ms Maywald said she wanted the reviewed plan to reflect that climate change is already well underway, rather than being a "future problem", and to put it front and centre.

Lock One in Blanchetown was constructed in 1922 to store water for consumptive use and to regulate river flows downstream. (ABC Open: Chris H)

She further wanted it to be less about "just add water", and use "smarter reform" to focus on reconnecting flood plains and wetlands, restoring fish passages, carp and invasive species management and coordinating land and water management.

"There will always be pressure to reopen settled science, reduce environmental commitments, or find new ways to delay delivery. Bowing to that pressure would be a serious mistake," Ms Maywald said.

"Climate change is accelerating every existing pressure across the system. Ecological resilience is weaker. Community patience is thinner. Institutional fatigue is real.

"This review will tell us whether we intend to finish the work, or quietly walk away from it."

Submissions for the 2026 Basin Plan Review close at 5pm May 1.

title: Twenty-five reasons Africa should be your ‘right now’ destination, not ‘one day’
text:
Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Our continent by continent expert update showcasing what’s hot and what’s not right across the world. See all stories .

With bookings on the rise, Africa is one of the most aspirational destinations. No longer just a “one day” vision board dream, the continent is enjoying a surge in Australian visitors. From the jungles of Rwanda to the ancient “Louvre of the Desert”, this extraordinary continent is finally calling us home. THE LOWDOWN Africa is big – bigger by far than it appears on most maps. This misrepresentation is the fault of the Mercator projection, a commonly used template developed by 16th century Flemish cartographer Gerardus Mercator, which supersizes some regions (we’re looking at you, Greenland) while shrinking the very cradle of humankind. Don’t be fooled by these “imperial” measurements: this behemoth of a continent accommodates a mind-boggling assortment of languages and ethnicities and a hefty share of topographical diversity. These ingredients are key to the travel trends identified by the African Travel and Tourism Association (ATTA): cultural immersion, heritage storytelling and conservation-led experiences. With bookings on the rise, according to a 2026 trends report by tour operator Collette, Africa is one of the most aspirational destinations. “Africa has always held a sense of awe and mystery,” says Collette director Roger Clulow. “For many travellers it’s a bucket-list destination and somewhere that they were never really sure they’d ever get to visit.” Cost has been an obstacle but rising prices elsewhere have levelled the “paying” field, according to Marco Rosa, co-founder of luxury boutique hotel specialist Plateau International. “In the past, Africa used to be much more expensive than other places, so you needed to set a higher budget. Now, it’s not because the cost of travel to Africa has gone down, it’s because all the other destinations have increased in price. So [for Australians travelling to] Africa, it’s levelling up.” Reason enough, for those still dilly-dallying, to cast off lingering doubts and turn long-held dreams into reality. Sign up for the Traveller Deals newsletter Get exclusive travel deals delivered straight to your inbox. Sign up now. WHAT’S HOT

Advertisement

More flight routes have put Ethiopia on Aussies’ radars … Great Danakil Depression, Mekelle. iStock Ethiopia is on the (literal) radar, with Ethiopian Airlines announcing plans to launch direct flights to Australia in 2028 pending the acquisition of suitable aircraft; two undisclosed Australian destination cities have been shortlisted and a new airport, expected to be Africa’s biggest when it opens in 2030, is also under construction 45 kilometres from the capital, Addis Ababa. Adventure travel is sizzling, and South Africa is one of the best places to hike, abseil and shipwreck-dive. Relaxation is also hot: last year nearly 1 million international visitors arrived in the Tanzanian archipelago of Zanzibar, an idyllic post-safari bolt-hole. WHAT’S NOT Let’s address the elephant in the room: safety. Avoiding the red “no-go” zones splotched on Smart Traveller’s map of Africa is a no-brainer. But you can still visit Jeffreys Bay, the Eastern Cape town where surfer Mick Fanning famously survived a shark attack, although the supertubes will be lacklustre: the Corona Cero Open J-Bay has been pulled from the World Surf League’s 2026 Championship Tour due to financial constraints. Then there’s the elephant no longer in the room: Craig, one of few remaining Big Tuskers, is being taxidermied after dying of old age in Amboseli National Park in January. The Kenyan Wildlife Authority hopes his preserved body will continue to underscore the ongoing risk of habitat loss, poaching, and climate change to Africa’s keystone species. MUST-VISIT COUNTRIES Botswana An elephant herd grazes by the Khwai River within the Okavango Delta iStock

Advertisement

Nature performs a miracle each year when water surges into the desert-swaddled Okavango Delta, transforming the floodplain into a latticework of channels, perfect for mokoro (dugout canoe) safaris. The delta remains soggy after the floodwaters have retreated, and wildlife can be seen congregating around the remaining waterholes. If you’ve “done” the delta, look beyond it to Makgadikgadi Pans, home to adorable meerkats. See benchafrica.com.au Seychelles Anse Source D’Argent beach, La Digue, Seychelles. iStock Flung like a handful of topaz-rimmed emeralds off East Africa’s coast and crawling with giant tortoises, this remote archipelago is reasserting its place on the map with new hotels and the launch of Aqua Expeditions’ luxury superyacht, the Aqua Lares. A cruise will take you to Astove Wall, an “underwater Grand Canyon” swaying with coral gardens and twitching with an unfathomable population of marine creatures. See aquaexpeditions.com Algeria Arch of Trajan located within the extensive Roman ruins of Timgad in Algeria. iStock When Crooked Compass founder Lisa Pagotto visited the Roman ruins of Timgad in 2023, her group of 11 “shared the entire ancient city with just a handful of other visitors. On that same day, Pompeii was announcing daily visitor caps of 22,000 as part of its sustainable tourism strategy.” The obscure site is one of seven in Algeria recognised by UNESCO. Visitors can expect “genuine cultural connection, while also allowing tourism to support a local economy that’s rebuilding [after the civil war of 1991 to 2002],” says Pagotto. See crooked-compass.com

Advertisement

Mozambique Aman Karingani is set to open. Aman You know a country’s on the move when Aman selects it as the destination for its first African safari lodge. The opening date for Aman Karingani in Karingani Game Reserve is under wraps. Meanwhile, take advantage of the new online e-visa portal and pair a safari in Gorongosa National Park with a surf break amid the white beaches and towering dunes of Bazaruto Archipelago National Park. See classicsafaricompany.com.au; evisa.gov.mz Sierra Leone Tiwai Island Wildlife Sanctuary on the Moa River in Sierra Leone. Alamy Stock Photo This West African nation is poised to take a bite of the tourism pie with the launch of Intrepid’s Sierra Leone adventure and UNESCO’s announcement of the country’s first world heritage site, Gola-Tiwai Complex; it encompasses Gola Rainforest National Park and Tiwai Island Wildlife Sanctuary, home to pygmy hippopotami and chimpanzees. The island is on Intrepid’s annual Not Hot List, which showcases lesser-known, tourism-ready destinations. See intrepidtravel.com MUST-SEE CITIES

Advertisement

Cape Town, South Africa Light and airy: The Claremont. “We want people to start exploring Cape Town from another angle,” says Jacques Smit of the Sabi Sabi Collection, which recently opened The Claremont Boutique Hotel near the mountain’s eastern slopes. “There’s more than 100 ways to hike to the top of Table Mountain National Park.” Look beyond the mountain to Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden, Muizenberg Beach and Constantia, home to the country’s oldest vineyards. See claremontboutiquehotel.com; capetown.travel Mombasa, Kenya Shree Parshva Vallabh Jain Temple in Mombasa, Kenya. iStock The world’s best chilli crab is served at Tamarind Mombasa, say those in the know. Dawa, Kenya’s signature cocktail, is also on the menu in this historic coastal trading city. Between feasts, wander streets shaped by African, Arab and European ancestry and skim tropical bays on a dhow while stopping over on Azamara’s 17-night Eastern & Southern Africa Cruise. See azamara.com Accra, Ghana

title: What style of wine − and how many bottles − should you take to a dinner party?
text:
Assuming it’s OK to take wine to a dinner party (some hosts may be offended), my recommendation is to take one bottle, not three. If your friends are thirsty types, though, three might, indeed, be the way to go.

What to choose? Ideally, suit the wine to the meal, your host and the other guests. Take a hearty shiraz if it’s a wintertime barbecue or a semillon if it’s a summertime seafood alfresco deal.

If your host has a show-off cellar, you can’t possibly compete, so take something eccentric, such as a Hungarian harslevelu or Georgian mtsvane. A bottle of Grange will always be a hit if your pockets are that deep.

title: Watch: Dyson Daniels ejected for elbow after fiery NBA brawl
text:
Advertisement

SportBasketballNBA Watch: Dyson Daniels ejected for elbow after fiery NBA brawl Jon Pierik May 1, 2026 — 12:05pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Australian basketball star Dyson Daniels and his Atlanta Hawks have shown some fight, but it ended in ejections on a night the New York Knicks produced a record-breaking performance to advance into the second round of the NBA play-offs on Friday (AEST). Frustrations boiled over with 4:39 minutes left in the second quarter of game six of their first-round playoff series at State Farm Arena when Daniels and Knicks centre Mitchell Robinson became entangled during a set of free throws. Loading With the Hawks trailing by an all-time play-offs record 47 points, Robinson took umbrage when Daniels appeared to elbow him, prompting Robinson to get into the face of the dogged guard. Players and officials rushed over to separate the pair. The fight spilled over into the front row of fans, appearing to involve most of the on-court members of both teams.

Advertisement

Related Article NBL ‘Deeply troubling’: Basketball’s battle of the billionaires reignites After the referees reviewed footage, Daniels and Robinson were given technical fouls and ejected from the game. During the skirmish, one of the referees fell to the floor. Veteran commentator Mike Breen, calling the action on ESPN, quickly noted Robinson’s frustration. “Oh, and Robinson and Dyson just being held up … Brett Barneke gets knocked down! Robinson wants to go after somebody,” he said. “They’re trying to separate the two. Robinson really upset. The frustrations coming through for the Hawks right now.”

Advertisement

It was a dirty night for the Hawks, who trailed 3-2 in the series and had been trying to keep their season alive. The Knicks led 83–36 at halftime, the largest half-time deficit ever in an NBA playoff game, and went on to win 140-89. This was the largest score by the Knicks in play-off history. Tough guys: Australian star Dyson Daniels and New York Knicks centre Mitchell Robinson get up close and personal in game six of their play-off series. Both men were ejected. AP Centre Karl Anthony-Towns had a triple double of 12 points, 11 rebounds and 10 assists, OG Anunoby had a team-high 29 points, while playmaker Jalen Brunson had 17 points and eight assists. Daniels had three points in 15 minutes before his ejection. Guard Jalen Johnson led the Hawks with 21 points, while star shooter CJ McCollum, the hero of games two and three, was held to only 11 points on 4-13 shooting from the floor.

Advertisement

The Knicks play the winner of the series between Boston and Philadelphia. Game six is later on Friday (AEST). News, results and expert analysis from the weekend of sport are sent every Monday. Sign up for our Sport newsletter.
